caveats: a: The staffing source is four-digit rather than six-digit.
a: Task exposure is inferred, not observed.
a: The supplied compensation ratio blends factory, engineering, sales, and support work with different automation potential.
rho: The sources describe smart manufacturing broadly, not realized savings in 334513.
rho: Safety integrity and customer qualification requirements vary materially by end market.
e: No source directly measures lens eligibility.
e: NAICS classifies establishments rather than consolidated firms.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and may include subsidiaries or firms whose sustainable EBITDA differs from the bridge assumption.
s5: The proxy is cross-industry, owner-level, and based on 2018 data.
s5: Owner age does not establish sale intent or transferability.
s5: Strategic subsidiaries do not follow individual-owner succession patterns.
q: No public source directly measures AI-benefit retention.
q: Capture can be much lower for standardized commodity sensors and higher for proprietary installed-base systems.
d5: BLS measures a broader four-digit industry.
d5: Technology benefits and government R&D priorities do not directly measure purchase demand.
d5: Quality adjustment is difficult when digital functionality expands rapidly.
o: Soft sensors and equipment-vendor integration could shift value away from standalone manufacturers.
o: The accountable operator may change from an independent instrument maker to a larger automation platform provider.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Instrument and system sales to process manufacturers, OEMs, distributors, engineering contractors, and industrial facilities, with repeat demand from production programs, installed-base replacement, calibration, consumables, software, configuration, and technical support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal operations, shells, financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, pure distributors or integrators classified elsewhere, and businesses without repeat external-customer revenue.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of instruments and related devices that measure, display, record, transmit, or control industrial process variables and repeatedly serve external industrial customers, including associated calibration, configuration, engineering, software, and support.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment for four-digit NAICS 334500 rather than wage-weighted AI task exposure in 334513. I translate its measured mix—17.58% architecture and engineering, 26.89% production, and 7.77% office support, plus management, computing, and sales—into task exposure: high for documentation, software assistance, quotation, planning, support, and inspection analytics; low for physical assembly, calibration, testing, and accountable process-safety decisions. Other 334500 industries may have different regulatory and production mixes, so the proxy can err in either direction.
caveats: The staffing source is four-digit rather than six-digit.
Task exposure is inferred, not observed.
The supplied compensation ratio blends factory, engineering, sales, and support work with different automation potential.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation table and wage-weighted task audit across engineering, applications, calibration, quality, customer support, and operations at private 334513 firms would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Process-instrument makers have substantial engineering, embedded-software, application-support, quality, documentation, and planning work amenable to AI assistance, but physical manufacture, precision calibration, validation, field troubleshooting, and safety-relevant approval remain resistant.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for four-digit NAICS 334500 at a 2.1% annual compound rate from 2024 to 2034; compounding that rate for five years gives approximately 1.11. I use it as a quantity-demand proxy for 334513, supported qualitatively by NIST and DOE evidence that smart manufacturing relies on sensor networks, computerized controls, advanced sensing, and real-time analytics. The broad BLS category includes medical, navigation, and laboratory instruments, so its growth may differ materially from industrial process instruments.
caveats: BLS measures a broader four-digit industry.
Technology benefits and government R&D priorities do not directly measure purchase demand.
Quality adjustment is difficult when digital functionality expands rapidly.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price US shipment and order forecasts for pressure, flow, level, temperature, analytical-process, and control instruments split by new capacity, retrofit, replacement, and service would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Industrial digitization, replacement of aging process equipment, energy and productivity programs, additional sensing, and demand for real-time control support moderate real growth. Industrial capital cycles, offshoring, component constraints, long installed lives, and software consolidation create downside.

caveats: No source directly measures lens eligibility.
NAICS classifies establishments rather than consolidated firms.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and may include subsidiaries or firms whose sustainable EBITDA differs from the bridge assumption.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the provided LMM population showing independent ownership, external revenue, repeat-order share, aftermarket/service mix, customer concentration, and standalone systems would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The classification is oriented to products for external industrial users, and repeat OEM orders, calibration, replacement, spares, software, and support can satisfy the lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive subsidiaries, project-only firms, commodity device makers without service recurrence, customer-concentrated businesses, and operations not transferable independently of a parent or founder.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 334513 ($5,208,062,981) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 334513 ($12,863,198,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $372,081 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334513); class <5: 284 firms, avg revenue/firm $652,007 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $165,088 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 114 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,700,877 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $430,662 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 110 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,473,282 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $879,435 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 163 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,363,644 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,624,075 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,237,528 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,454,142 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 55 firms, avg revenue/firm $145,730,982 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,899,085 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 163

caveats: Soft sensors and equipment-vendor integration could shift value away from standalone manufacturers.
The accountable operator may change from an independent instrument maker to a larger automation platform provider.
o.change_evidence: End-user and OEM evidence on instrument functions eliminated by soft sensors, embedded controls, self-calibration, and vertically integrated equipment—versus functions still bought from specialist suppliers—would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Industrial processes still require accountable suppliers to engineer, manufacture, calibrate, certify, warrant, secure, and support physical sensing and control devices. AI and software may automate analysis and migrate some functions into integrated platforms, but cannot eliminate the need to measure and actuate physical variables at the process boundary.

caveats: No public source directly measures AI-benefit retention.
Capture can be much lower for standardized commodity sensors and higher for proprietary installed-base systems.
q.change_evidence: Contract and transaction-level evidence on cost-down clauses, price-review cadence, bid competition, gross-margin persistence, qualification duration, and customer switching would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Qualification records, installed-base compatibility, process know-how, calibration traceability, switching costs, and failure consequences can let differentiated instrument makers retain productivity benefits. Competitive tenders, large industrial buyers, distributor pressure, negotiated OEM cost-downs, and eventual product repricing still pass part of the benefit through.

caveats: The sources describe smart manufacturing broadly, not realized savings in 334513.
Safety integrity and customer qualification requirements vary materially by end market.
rho.change_evidence: Verified deployments at small and midsize process-instrument manufacturers showing labor hours, validation effort, error rates, adoption, and sustained use by workflow would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Engineering copilots, automated documentation, quote support, production planning, service triage, and AI-assisted inspection can be deployed within five years. Realization is moderated by heterogeneous legacy data, industrial cybersecurity, long product qualification cycles, traceability, metrology, explainability, and the need for reliable human approval in high-stakes process environments.

s5.bridge: Census reports 51% of responding owners of US employer businesses across industries were at least age 55 in 2018, not transfers in 334513. I discount that owner-age pool for institutional ownership, multiple owners, delayed retirement, family succession, internal reorganizations, closures, failed deals, and nontransferable technical operations. The proxy may overstate transfer propensity if industrial instrument firms are disproportionately corporate subsidiaries, or understate it if founder succession is unusually concentrated.
caveats: The proxy is cross-industry, owner-level, and based on 2018 data.
Owner age does not establish sale intent or transferability.
Strategic subsidiaries do not follow individual-owner succession patterns.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, or Axial transaction records mapped to 334513 plus a private-company owner-age and succession survey would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Owner aging implies a material succession pool, but only a minority of eligible firms should complete an external qualifying control transfer within five years.

principal_driver: Durable physical measurement and control demand combined with repeat installed-base revenue and automatable engineering workflows.
principal_weakness: The main quantitative evidence is a four-digit proxy, and lens eligibility among product manufacturers is not directly measured.

caveats: S1 is cited for a even though it reports industry output rather than occupation mix or task exposure; S2 supplies the occupation shares, while the conversion to a remains an openly labeled four-digit proxy.
S3 establishes the product-class boundary but not the numerical e, q, or o estimates; those values remain disclosed judgments rather than source observations.
S4, S5, and S7 are broad smart-manufacturing evidence and do not observe workflow-level AI exposure, five-year realization, or supplier demand in NAICS 334513.
S6 measures cross-industry owner age in 2018, not qualifying control transfers or sale intent in 334513; the s5 bridge discloses this limitation and plausible error directions.
Every source access date is 2026-07-23, one day after the packet run date and mandated review date; this is a metadata inconsistency, not a substantive contradiction in the reopened sources.

sources_audited.note: The BLS table directly reports NAICS 334500 chained-dollar output of 149.7 in 2024, 184.7 in 2034, and a 2.1% compound annual growth rate, supporting the stated d5 proxy. It does not itself support the separate task-exposure inference for a.
The May 2023 OEWS table for NAICS 334500 directly reports architecture and engineering at 17.58%, production at 26.89%, and office and administrative support at 7.77% of employment.

The Census NAICS archive directly defines 334513 and lists the stated industrial process variables, but it does not measure recurring external revenue, lens eligibility, AI-benefit retention, or operator survival.

The NIST roadmap supports the stated broad opportunities in analytics, sensing, autonomy, digital twins, robotics, and operations and the cited barriers involving heterogeneous systems, explainability, reliability, and safety. It does not provide 334513-specific adoption or realized-savings evidence.

The NIST article supports qualitative claims about industrial AI, sensing, cybersecurity, adaptive control, product-quality monitoring, embedded and secondary sensors, and human interaction. Its experimental smart-manufacturing context does not establish 334513-specific exposure, realization, demand, or displacement magnitudes.
The Census visualization reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older for data year 2018 and expressly limits the population as described in the packet.

The DOE article supports the qualitative statement that smart manufacturing uses networks of advanced sensors, data analytics, and process controls for real-time control of productivity, energy, and costs. It does not measure demand growth for 334513 suppliers.
